source_intake_record.raw_question_text: It is believed that nucleons $(\mathrm{N})$ interact directly through the weak interaction and that the latter violates parity conservation. One way to study the nature of the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}$ weak interaction is by means of $\alpha$-decay, as typified by the decays of the $3^{+}, T=1$ and $3^{-}, T=0$ states of ${ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ (Fig. 2.29). In the following you will be asked to explain the principles of an experiment to measure the weak-interaction matrix element between these states, $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$. There is also a $1^{+} / 1^{-}$parity doublet at $\sim 11.23 \mathrm{MeV}$. Both states have $T=1$. As in part(a), which isospin components of the weak N-N interaction contribute to the mixing matrix element? (Note that ${ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ is self-conjugate) Which would be determined by a measurement of the parity-forbidden $\alpha$ width?

answer is short, but the prompt structure suggests more than one conceptual inference step

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: It is believed that nucleons $(\mathrm{N})$ interact directly through the weak interaction and that the latter violates parity conservation. One way to study the nature of the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}$ weak interaction is by means of $\alpha$-decay, as typified by the decays of the $3^{+}, T=1$ and $3^{-}, T=0$ states of ${ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ (Fig. 2.29). In the following you will be asked to explain the principles of an experiment to measure the weak-interaction matrix element between these states, $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$. As in part(a), which isospin components of the weak N-N interaction contribute to the mixing matrix element? metadata.reasoning_chain: As ${ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ is a self-conjugate nucleus, $T_{3}=0$ because $\langle 1,0 \mid 1,0 ; 1,0\rangle=$ 0 . So only the components of $T=0,2$ can contribute. However in weak interaction, $|\Delta T| \leq 1$, and so only the component with $\Delta T=0$ can contribute to the experiment result.

open_ended_problem_variants.rewritten_question_text: It is believed that nucleons $(\mathrm{N})$ interact directly through the weak interaction and that the latter violates parity conservation. One way to study the nature of the $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}$ weak interaction is by means of $\alpha$-decay, as typified by the decays of the $3^{+}, T=1$ and $3^{-}, T=0$ states of ${ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ (Fig. 2.29). In the following you will be asked to explain the principles of an experiment to measure the weak-interaction matrix element between these states, $\left\langle 3^{+}\right| H_{\text {weak }}\left|3^{-}\right\rangle$. There is also a $1^{+} / 1^{-}$ parity doublet at $\sim 11.23 \mathrm{MeV}$. Both states have $T=1$. As in part (a), which isospin components of the weak N-N interaction contribute to the mixing matrix element? (Note that ${ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ is self-conjugate.) Which would be determined by a measurement of the parity-forbidden $\alpha$ width?
